description:
You are a DevSecOps engineer tasked with enforcing “policy-as-code” for Python environments.  
Create an auditable snapshot of a *brand-new* virtual environment.

Target end-state that the automated grader will verify:

1.  A directory called  
   /home/user/security_scan  
   must exist.

2.  Inside that directory there must be a Python virtual environment that was
    created *in place* (i.e. the usual structure containing “bin/activate”, “lib/python…”, etc.).

3.  Still inside that directory, create a text file named  
   /home/user/security_scan/initial_requirements.txt

   • The file must be generated **only** by running  
     `pip freeze --all` (or `pip freeze`) while the virtual environment is activated.  
   • Save the entire, unedited console output into the file (one requirement per line,
     exactly as pip prints them, ending with a single trailing newline).

4.  For automated marking, the file **must** contain at least the following three lines
    (the actual version numbers will vary, that is fine):

        pip==<some_version>
        setuptools==<some_version>
        wheel==<some_version>

    “pip freeze” prints results alphabetically by package name, so these three lines
    will normally appear in that order, but the grader only requires that
    all three lines exist, each starting with the exact package name followed by
    “==”.

That’s it.  
A correct solution only needs the small sequence of typical commands:

• create the directory and the virtual environment,  
• activate the venv,  
• run pip freeze and redirect the output to the specified file.

Do **not** add or remove any packages before running `pip freeze`; the goal is to
capture the pristine state produced by `python -m venv` (which includes pip,
setuptools and wheel by default).

When you are done you should have exactly:

- /home/user/security_scan/ (directory)
    ├── bin/ … (virtual-env standard content)
    ├── lib/ … (virtual-env standard content)
    └── initial_requirements.txt  (freeze snapshot)

The grader will:

a) confirm that the directory exists and is recognisable as a Python virtual
   environment (e.g. it contains “bin/activate”), and  
b) open initial_requirements.txt and verify that it contains lines beginning
   with “pip==”, “setuptools==” and “wheel==”.

Make sure all paths and filenames match **exactly** as stated above.
truth:
The automated tests will:

1. Assert that the directory /home/user/security_scan exists and contains
   an “activate” script at /home/user/security_scan/bin/activate.

2. Read /home/user/security_scan/initial_requirements.txt and confirm:

   • The file exists and is not empty.  
   • It contains a line that starts with “pip==”.  
   • It contains a line that starts with “setuptools==”.  
   • It contains a line that starts with “wheel==”.

   (The exact versions are not checked.)

No additional hidden files or requirements are necessary for this task.
